summary: Fowler argues that academia's emphasis on publication history actively discourages researchers from gaining practical industry experience, creating a harmful divide. A post-doc explained that spending time in industry would create a fatal gap in his publication record, ruining his academic job prospects. Fowler contends that mining effective techniques from real-world software development experience is valuable academic work, but is dismissed because it lacks novelty. He proposes that academic departments should include faculty with substantial industry experience and encourage regular rotation between academic and industrial roles to improve knowledge transfer and make research more practically relevant.
key_points: Academia penalizes researchers who take industry positions by viewing gaps in publication history as career-ending
The patterns community's focus on documenting proven techniques is dismissed academically because it lacks novelty
Drawing lessons from practical experience should be considered valuable academic work but is currently devalued
Even highly accomplished industry engineers struggle to obtain meaningful academic positions
Regular rotation between industry and academia would improve knowledge transfer and research relevance
money_quotes: A year or two in industry would create a gap in your publication history that would be lethal to your job prospects.
The academics I respect are, I'm told, not highly regarded within academia because the things that I count as useful are usually dismissed by the academic community.
My work, for example, is generally dismissed because all I do is write about stuff that is old hat.
By devaluing it the academic world is ignoring a fruitful avenue to improve the capabilities of our profession.
I find it hard to believe that students wouldn't gain an enormous amount from being taught by people with a long and thoughtful experience in the profession they are entering.
People are the key to good knowledge transfer.
key_insight: Academia's obsession with novelty and publication records creates perverse incentives that prevent researchers from gaining the practical experience needed to produce genuinely useful research.
